expectations.text: Explains that heavier loads increase metabolic/oxygen cost per set, requiring longer recovery — this is a normal progression
expectations.evidence: Section 1 explains 'Higher force production → greater motor unit recruitment,' 'Greater metabolic demand → faster depletion of phosphocreatine,' and 'More CNS fatigue.' States this is 'a normal and expected phenomenon.'

expectations.text: Notes research on rest intervals for hypertrophy is mixed (some favor longer, some shorter, some no difference)
expectations.evidence: The answer presents shorter rest as clearly advantageous for hypertrophy: 'Shorter rest periods are actually advantageous for hypertrophy.' Does not present the research as mixed — Greg said findings are mixed with some studies favoring longer, shorter, or no difference.

expectations.text: For strength, leans toward longer rest periods, especially for strong lifters on compound lifts
expectations.evidence: 'Strength Training: Optimal rest: 3–5 minutes. The research is clear: for maximal strength development, longer rest periods are superior.' Cites multiple studies.

expectations.text: Provides concrete rest period ranges (e.g., ~90s-2min for isolation/upper, ~3min common, ~5min+ for heavy compounds)
expectations.evidence: Specific ranges: '60–70% 1RM: 60–90 seconds,' '80–90% 1RM: 2–3 minutes,' '90%+ 1RM: 3–5+ minutes.' Practical section: 'Expect 3–5 minutes between heavy deadlift sets.'

expectations.text: Suggests non-interfering supersets as a time-saving strategy
expectations.evidence: No mention of supersets anywhere in the answer. Greg specifically suggested non-interfering supersets to save time without sacrificing performance.
